text: Hi, this is Dr. Augustino Cervone. I produced this video for patient education to help explain what it's like to perform a lysis of adhesions.
The surgeon uses robotic arms to set up surgical instruments in preparation for performing adhesiolysis.
This is a patient who has had previous surgery and now has an abdominal hernia. we'll be repairing the hernia.
The surgeon begins the procedure by carefully dissecting and removing scar tissue from the abdominal cavity, which was left behind from previous surgeries.
This can also happen with previous inflammation, like an infection.
The camera is introduced into the abdomen, revealing normal intestinal contents visible within the abdominal cavity.
The camera view reveals the ceiling of the abdomen, showing evidence of previous surgical intervention with visible scar tissue.
The surgeon addresses scarring on the anterior abdominal wall, which is suspending the omentum and intestines.
The surgeon carefully dissects through the scar tissue, with the expectation of revealing the intestines as the procedure progresses.
The surgeon uses fine scissors and occasional cautery to carefully dissect through scar tissue within the abdominal cavity, revealing underlying structures.
The surgeon carefully dissects through adhesions to free the anterior abdominal wall, revealing the underlying tissue structures.
The nice advantage of the robot is that I am peering into the abdomen using high definition imagery as well as the images are three dimensional.
So each of my moves are intentional. As much as I can't feel what
tension I'm putting on tissue, it becomes a sense of understanding how the tissue surrounding what you're dissecting reacts.
So you get an impression of how much pulling or tugging is enough, too little, or too much.
The surgeon releases adhesions from the anterior abdominal wall, using either manual dissection or cautery to separate the fat tissue quickly.
The surgeon approaches the intestines, navigating the surgical field with increased caution due to the proximity to these delicate structures.
We try to avoid injuring the intestines obviously, but on occasion this is inadvertent and in which case may require repair of the intestines.
The key to performing a lysis of adhesions without injury is moving slowly.

Now as much as this can be done through an open incision, as well as done laparoscopically, performing this robotically, we tend to have a steadier hand and are able to visualize almost individual strands of the scar tissue.
As you can see, this becomes a tedious process. It requires patience and certainly the skill in developing these tissue planes.
I will also say that as much as we expect there to be adhesions in a patient who has had previous operations, certainly somebody with an abdominal wall hernia, we would expect adhesions.
We can never tell without actually looking in there what's involved, what's going to be involved, how much time is needed, and what's to be expected.

No matter if somebody has had CAT scans, ultrasounds, other studies that didn't involve us being in the abdomen, it's impossible to tell beforehand. And as you can see, this is a process that can take a period of time.
And certainly, it can take longer than the actual repair of the hernia.
And from a surgeon's perspective, the longer time we're in the operating room performing any portion of an operation, the more fatigue that we encounter.
As you saw at the beginning of this video, in performing robotic surgery, the surgeon sits behind a console. This limits the amount of fatigue.
I would also add that comfort of the surgeon potentially translates into better outcomes for the patient.

And my reason for saying that is that when performing robotic surgery, the surgeon is relatively comfortable behind the console. And taking it to a sports analogy, the surgeon gets into his or her zone, so to speak, when performing robotics.
Well, I hope you've enjoyed my little video on license of adhesions, and hopefully it's created some clarity.
